symptoms.symptom_name: Violaceous, flat-topped papules on flexor wrists and ankles

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Colour, flat-topped shape and flexural distribution are the classic morphology of cutaneous lichen planus, discernible even by patient description.
Discrete flat-topped papules are unlike the confluent eczematous plaques of contact dermatitis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Wickham striae — fine white reticulate lines on the papule surface

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Wickham striae are near-pathognomonic for lichen planus but require close inspection or dermoscopy — sealed as a clinician-only finding not obtainable in telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Severe intractable pruritus, worse at night, with sleep disruption

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pruritus is characteristic of lichen planus but non-specific.
Night-worse itch is a scabies feature — keeps scabies on the differential until morphology/burrow screen excludes it.
Broken skin is the risk factor and portal for secondary infection — the excoriation is the safety concern, not a diagnostic pointer for the primary dermatosis.
Scratch damage reflects itch severity, not the underlying cause.

symptoms.symptom_name: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation in older, resolving lesions

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Resolution with brown post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation is typical of lichen planus lesions.
Burrows in finger web spaces are the hallmark of scabies; their absence on close exam argues strongly against it — a clinician-only finding.

symptoms.symptom_name: No new soaps, detergents, cosmetics or topical exposures

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A negative exposure history reduces the likelihood of a contact-allergic cause.
A lichenoid drug eruption is clinically identical; a clear negative medication history makes it unlikely.

symptoms.symptom_name: No spreading erythema, warmth, purulent exudate or fever

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of heat, spread, purulence and fever excludes established secondary infection at this moment — but it must be re-screened as the key safety-net.

symptoms.symptom_name: No respiratory symptoms (no breathlessness, wheeze or throat tightness)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: No systemic or airway allergic features, and fixed 3-day papules rather than transient wheals, exclude an acute allergic/anaphylactic process.

symptoms.symptom_name: Afebrile with otherwise normal vital signs

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Clinician-measured normal observations (BP 128/78, HR 88, Temp 36.9, RR 16, SpO2 100%) support the absence of systemic infection — sealed as a clinician measurement; the AI infers 'no fever' only from patient report.

symptoms.symptom_name: No oral or genital mucosal involvement

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Mucosal involvement would broaden the disease and change management/surveillance; its absence keeps this a purely cutaneous, milder presentation.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Violaceous flat-topped papules + Wickham striae + resolution with hyperpigmentation in a flexural distribution is a near-diagnostic pattern for cutaneous lichen planus.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Dermoscopy of a representative papule (to visualise Wickham striae)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Non-invasive in-person confirmation of the near-pathognomonic surface reticulation; not obtainable via telehealth.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Punch skin biopsy (H&E, with direct immunofluorescence if atypical)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Confirmatory when the diagnosis is uncertain or atypical; for a classic mild case it is optional and can await GP/dermatology review rather than being urgent.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Skin scraping and microscopy for scabies mite/eggs
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not required given classic lichenoid morphology and no burrows; reserve only for genuine diagnostic doubt to avoid unnecessary testing and empirical scabicide.
No current signs of secondary infection; swab and treat only if the wounds later become hot, swollen or purulent.
No systemic features — routine bloods would not change management of a mild localised dermatosis.
